translations.literal: In the reply submitted before, the strip threading through was not finished, the binding thread was not ended, the wording was not distinguished clear, the points were not distinct and bright—this is your servant’s shallow and mean crime.
translations.idiomatic: My first answer trailed off, tangled its thread, blurred its terms—that was my fault for shallow learning.

sentences.zh: 冊曰：「善言天者必有征於人，善言古者必有驗於今。」

translations.literal: The document said: “He who speaks well of Heaven must have evidence in man; he who speaks well of antiquity must have verification in the present.”
translations.idiomatic: Your text said, “Explain Heaven on the evidence of man; prove antiquity by what we see today.”

sentences.zh: 臣聞天者群物之祖也。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that Heaven is the ancestor of the throng of things.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven is father to the myriad things.

sentences.zh: 故遍覆包涵而無所殊，建日月風雨以和之，經陰陽寒暑以成之。

translations.literal: Therefore it everywhere covers, wraps, and contains them without distinguishing, establishes sun, moon, wind, and rain to harmonize them, threads yin and yang, cold and heat to complete them.
translations.idiomatic: It roofs all creation without partiality, sets sun and moon and wind and rain to tune them, and weaves cold and heat through yin and yang to ripen them.

sentences.zh: 故聖人法天而立道，亦溥愛而亡私，布德施仁以厚之，設誼立禮以導之。

translations.literal: Therefore the sage models Heaven and establishes the Way; he also broadly loves without selfishness, spreads virtue and extends benevolence to enrich them, sets up righteousness and establishes ritual to guide them.
translations.idiomatic: The sage takes Heaven as his model: boundless care without private favor, kindness to feed the people, duty and rites to lead them.

sentences.zh: 春者天之所以生也，仁者君之所以愛也；

translations.literal: Spring is that whereby Heaven gives birth; benevolence is that whereby the lord cherishes.
translations.idiomatic: Spring is Heaven’s birth-gift; humaneness is how the ruler loves.

sentences.zh: 夏者天之所以長也，德者君之所以養也；

translations.literal: Summer is that whereby Heaven grows; virtue is that whereby the lord nurtures.
translations.idiomatic: Summer is Heaven’s growth; nurturing character is the ruler’s part.

sentences.zh: 霜者天之所以殺也，刑者君之所以罰也。

translations.literal: Frost is that whereby Heaven kills; punishment is that whereby the lord fines.
translations.idiomatic: Frost is Heaven’s killing edge; penalties are how the ruler checks wrong.

sentences.zh: 繇此言之，天人之征，古今之道也。

translations.literal: From this speaking, the sign of Heaven and man is the way of antiquity and present.
translations.idiomatic: Thus the echo between Heaven and man is the same bridge from past to present.

sentences.zh: 孔子作《春秋》，上揆之天道，下質諸人情，參之於古，考之於今。

translations.literal: Confucius made the Chunqiu, above matching it to Heaven’s Way, below interrogating it in human feeling, joining it to antiquity, examining it in the present.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn Annals with one eye on Heaven’s pattern, one on human passion, checked against old ages and today.

sentences.zh: 故《春秋》之所譏，災害之所加也；

translations.literal: Therefore what the Chunqiu ridicules is where disaster and harm attach;
translations.idiomatic: What the Annals mocks, Heaven strikes;

sentences.zh: 《春秋》之所惡，怪異之所施也。

translations.literal: what the Chunqiu hates is where the strange and anomalous apply.
translations.idiomatic: what the Annals condemns, portents visit.

sentences.zh: 書邦家之過，兼災異之變；

translations.literal: It records the faults of states and houses, together with the changes of disasters and prodigies;
translations.idiomatic: It inscribes the failings of houses and kingdoms beside their calamities and omens;

sentences.zh: 以此見人之所為，其美惡之極，乃與天地流通而往來相應，此亦言天之一端也。

translations.literal: by this is seen that what men do, at the utmost of their beauty and ugliness, flows and interchanges with Heaven and earth and responds back and forth—this also is one end of speaking of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: so we see human conduct, pushed to its best or worst, trades breath with Heaven and earth—another way of “speaking Heaven.”

sentences.zh: 古者修教訓之官，務以德善化民，民已大化之後，天下常亡一人之獄矣。

translations.literal: In antiquity there were officials who cultivated instruction and training, striving with virtue and goodness to transform the people; after the people were greatly transformed, all under Heaven constantly had not one man in prison.
translations.idiomatic: Antiquity set officers to teach, moved the people by goodness until, fully transformed, the empire often held not a single prisoner.

sentences.zh: 今世廢而不修，亡以化民，民以故棄行誼而死財利，是以犯法而罪多，一歲之獄以萬千數。

translations.literal: In the present age it is cast aside and not cultivated; there is nothing wherewith to transform the people; the people therefore cast aside conduct and righteousness and die for wealth and profit—therefore they violate the law and crimes are many; in one year’s prisons they are in the tens of thousands.
translations.idiomatic: We let that office rot, so the people ditch duty for profit, break the law in droves, and fill the jails by tens of thousands a year.

sentences.zh: 以此見古之不可不用也，故《春秋》變古則譏之。

translations.literal: By this is seen that antiquity cannot be not used; therefore the Chunqiu, when antiquity is changed, ridicules it.
translations.idiomatic: The Annals jeer whenever a state abandons the old model—Heaven’s lesson is that the ancient machinery must be used.

sentences.zh: 天令之謂命，命非聖人不行；

translations.literal: What Heaven commands is called fate; fate does not move without the sage;
translations.idiomatic: Heaven’s command is fate, and fate does not stir without a sage;

sentences.zh: 質樸之謂性，性非教化不成；

translations.literal: what is plain and simple is called nature; nature does not complete without transformation through instruction;
translations.idiomatic: the raw endowment is nature, and nature is not finished without teaching;

sentences.zh: 人欲之謂情，情非度制不節。

translations.literal: what man desires is called feeling; feeling is not restrained without measure and regulation.
translations.idiomatic: appetite is feeling, and feeling runs wild without measure and law.

sentences.zh: 是故王者上謹於承天意，以順命也；

translations.literal: Therefore the king, above, is careful in receiving Heaven’s intent, thereby to comply with fate;
translations.idiomatic: So the true king above heeds Heaven’s mind to answer his mandate;

sentences.zh: 下務明教化民，以成性也；

translations.literal: below he strives to clarify transformation through instruction for the people, thereby to complete nature;
translations.idiomatic: below he clarifies moral teaching to perfect human nature;

sentences.zh: 正法度之宜，別上下之序，以防欲也；

translations.literal: he corrects what is fitting in laws and measures, distinguishes the order of high and low, thereby to guard desire;
translations.idiomatic: he sets just laws, marks rank high and low, and fences desire;

sentences.zh: 修此三者，而大本舉矣。

translations.literal: cultivate these three, then the great root is raised.
translations.idiomatic: do those three and the great root is planted.

sentences.zh: 人受命於天，固超然異於群生，入有父子兄弟之親，出有君臣上下之誼，會聚相遇，則有耆老長幼之施，粲然有文以相接，歡然有恩以相愛，此人之所以貴也。

translations.literal: Man receives the mandate from Heaven; he is indeed loftily different from the throng of the living—within he has the closeness of father and son, elder and younger brothers; without he has the righteousness of lord and minister, high and low; when they meet and gather, there is the extending of old and young; brightly there is pattern by which to connect, gladly there is grace by which to love one another—this is why man is honored.
translations.idiomatic: Man, charged by Heaven, stands above the creatures: at home he has the ties of kin, abroad the order of ruler and subject; in company he shows respect for age; he moves in clear ritual and warm kindness—that is his nobility.

sentences.zh: 生五穀以食之，桑麻以衣之，六畜以養之，服牛乘馬，圈豹檻虎，是其得天之靈，貴於物也。

translations.literal: He grows the five grains to feed them, mulberry and hemp to clothe them, the six domestic animals to nurture them; he yokes oxen and rides horses, pens leopards and cages tigers—this is his obtaining Heaven’s numinous power and being honored above things.
translations.idiomatic: He grows grain for food, silk for clothes, herds for meat, harnesses oxen and horses, cages beasts—gifts of Heaven’s spirit that set him above the brutes.

sentences.zh: 故孔子曰：「天地之性人為貴。」

translations.literal: Therefore Confucius said: “Among the natures of Heaven and earth, man is honored.”
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, “Of all nature between Heaven and earth, none is nobler than man.”

sentences.zh: 明於天性，知自貴於物；

translations.literal: Clear on Heaven’s nature, he knows to honor himself above things;
translations.idiomatic: Knowing Heaven’s intent for man, he knows his own dignity;

sentences.zh: 知自貴於物，然後知仁誼；

translations.literal: knowing to honor himself above things, then he knows benevolence and righteousness;
translations.idiomatic: that dignity found, he grasps humaneness and right;

sentences.zh: 知仁誼，然後重禮節；

translations.literal: knowing benevolence and righteousness, then he esteems ritual and moderation;
translations.idiomatic: with humaneness and right, he cherishes ritual and measure;

sentences.zh: 重禮節，然後安處善；

translations.literal: esteeming ritual and moderation, then he peacefully dwells in the good;
translations.idiomatic: cherishing ritual, he rests in the good;

sentences.zh: 安處善，然後樂循理；

translations.literal: peacefully dwelling in the good, then he delights in following pattern;
translations.idiomatic: resting in the good, he takes joy in the Way;

sentences.zh: 樂循理，然後謂之君之。

translations.literal: delighting in following pattern, then he is called fit to be lord (gentleman).
translations.idiomatic: joying in the Way, he earns the name of gentleman.

sentences.zh: 故孔子曰「不知命，亡以為君子」，此之謂也。

translations.literal: Therefore Confucius said “Without knowing fate, there is no way to be a gentleman”—this speaks of it.
translations.idiomatic: Hence Confucius said, “Without knowing Heaven’s charge, one cannot be a gentleman”—he meant this ladder of self-cultivation.

sentences.zh: 冊曰：「上嘉唐、虞，下悼桀、紂，浸微浸滅浸明浸昌之道，虛心以改。」

translations.literal: The document said: “Above commending Tang and Yu, below grieving Jie and Zhou Xin, the way of gradually fading and gradually perishing, gradually brightening and gradually flourishing—with empty mind to reform.”
translations.idiomatic: Your rescript asked me to praise Yao and Shun, mourn Jie and Zhou, and trace how kingdoms wither or wax—so you may reform with an open heart.”

sentences.zh: 臣聞眾少成多，積小致臣，故聖人莫不以晻致明，以微致顯。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that the many are completed from the few, and the great is reached by accumulating the small; therefore the sage none does not use dimness to reach brightness, use the slight to reach the manifest.
translations.idiomatic: Many grains of sand make a tower; the sage turns faint beginnings into blazing success.

sentences.zh: 是以堯發於諸侯，舜興乎深山，非一日而顯也，蓋有漸以致之矣。

translations.literal: Therefore Yao rose from the marquises, Shun rose from deep mountains—not in one day did they become manifest; probably there was gradualness by which it was brought about.
translations.idiomatic: Yao rose from a fief, Shun from the wildwood—neither leaped to fame in a day; both climbed by slow degrees.

sentences.zh: 言出於已，不可塞也；

translations.literal: Words that issue from oneself cannot be stopped;
translations.idiomatic: A word once spoken cannot be chased home;

sentences.zh: 行發於身，不可掩也。

translations.literal: acts that issue from the body cannot be covered.
translations.idiomatic: a deed once done cannot be hidden.

sentences.zh: 言行，治之大者，君子之所以動天地也。

translations.literal: Words and acts are the greatest in ordering; they are how the gentleman moves Heaven and earth.
translations.idiomatic: Speech and conduct are government’s hinge—they are how a gentleman shakes Heaven and earth.

sentences.zh: 故盡小者大，慎微者著。

translations.literal: Therefore he who exhausts the small becomes great; he who is careful of the slight becomes illustrious.
translations.idiomatic: Mind the small to grow great, watch the subtle to shine bright.

sentences.zh: 《詩》云：「惟此文王，小心翼翼。」

translations.literal: The Odes says: “Only this King Wen, careful and cautious.”
translations.idiomatic: The Odes say of King Wen, “He walked in fear and care.”

sentences.zh: 胡堯兢兢日行其道，而舜業業日致其孝，善積而名顯，德章而身尊，以其浸明浸昌之道也。

translations.literal: Thus Yao daily walked his way in fear and trembling, and Shun daily extended his filial piety in earnest toil; goodness accumulated and name became manifest, virtue shone and the person was honored—by the way of gradually brightening and gradually flourishing.
translations.idiomatic: So Yao trod the Way with trembling care, Shun piled up filial deeds day by day—goodness stacked until their names shone, virtue plain until all honored them: the path of slow, sure growth.

sentences.zh: 積善在身，猶長日加益，而人不知也；

translations.literal: Goodness accumulated in the person is like the long day adding increase, yet people do not know it;
translations.idiomatic: Goodness in the body lengthens like a summer day—yet no one notices the stretch;

sentences.zh: 積惡在身，猶火之銷膏，而人不見也。

translations.literal: evil accumulated in the person is like fire melting fat, yet people do not see it.
translations.idiomatic: evil eats the marrow like fire on lard—yet the victim never sees the burn.

sentences.zh: 非明乎情性察乎流俗者，孰能知之？

translations.literal: Unless one is clear on feeling and nature and examines the floating customs, who can know it?
translations.idiomatic: Only a man who reads passion and probes fashion can see it coming.

sentences.zh: 此唐、虞之所以得令名，而桀、紂之可為悼懼者也。

translations.literal: This is how Tang and Yu obtained fine names, and how Jie and Zhou may be grieved and feared.
translations.idiomatic: That is how Yao and Shun won praise, and how Jie and Zhou became warnings.

sentences.zh: 夫善惡之相從，如景鄉之應形聲也。

translations.literal: As for good and evil following one another, it is like shadow and echo responding to form and sound.
translations.idiomatic: Good and evil trail each other as shadow and echo answer body and voice.

sentences.zh: 故桀、紂暴謾，讒賊並進，賢知隱伏，惡日顯，國日亂，晏然自以如日在天，終陵夷而大壞。

translations.literal: Therefore Jie and Zhou Xin were violent and slow; slanderous thieves advanced together; the worthy and wise hid and lay low; evil daily became manifest, the state daily chaotic; they were complacent as if like the sun in Heaven—finally they slid down and were greatly ruined.
translations.idiomatic: Jie and Zhou grew cruel and slack; flatterers swarmed while sages hid; evil mounted, the realm rotted, yet they lolled as if the sun would never set—then came the crash.

sentences.zh: 夫暴逆不仁者，非一日而亡也，亦以漸至，故桀、紂雖亡道，然猶享國十餘年，此其浸微浸滅之道也。

translations.literal: As for the violent, contrary, and inhumane, they do not perish in one day; it also arrives by gradualness—therefore although Jie and Zhou had lost the Way, still they enjoyed the state more than ten years: this is their way of gradually fading and gradually perishing.
translations.idiomatic: Tyrants do not fall overnight; Jie and Zhou clung to the throne years after the Way was gone—such is the slow slide to ruin.

sentences.zh: 冊曰：「三王之教所祖不同，而皆有失，或謂久而不易者道也，意豈異哉？」

translations.literal: The document said: “The teachings of the Three Kings, what they took as ancestor differed, yet each had loss; some say what long endures without change is the Way—is the intent perhaps different?”
translations.idiomatic: You also asked whether the Three Kings’ different models each failed, yet some call the unchanging core “the Way”—do those views clash?

sentences.zh: 臣聞夫樂而不亂復而不厭者謂之道；

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that what is joyful without disorder, repeated without satiety is called the Way;
translations.idiomatic: What stays ordered yet never cloys, returns yet never wearies—that is the Way;

sentences.zh: 道者萬世之弊，弊者道之失也。

translations.literal: the Way is the flaw of ten thousand generations; the flaw is the Way’s loss.
translations.idiomatic: the Way runs through all ages; “flaw” only means a moment when the Way slips.

sentences.zh: 先王之道必有偏而不起之處，故政有眊而不行，舉其偏者以補其弊而已矣。

translations.literal: The way of the former kings necessarily has places where it leans and does not rise; therefore government has dimness and does not go—raise its leaning part to repair its flaw, that is all.
translations.idiomatic: Every old program tilts somewhere; when policy dims, lift the weak side and patch the crack—nothing more.

sentences.zh: 三王之道所祖不同，非其相反，將以救溢扶衰，所遭之變然也。

translations.literal: The ways of the Three Kings, what they took as ancestor differed, not because they opposed one another, but because they intended to rescue overflow and support decline—the changes they encountered were so.
translations.idiomatic: The Three Kings differed because each age needed a different brace—not because their goals contradicted.

sentences.zh: 故孔子曰：「亡為而治者，其舜乎！」

translations.literal: Therefore Confucius said: “He who does nothing yet orders—perhaps only Shun!”
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, “To rule without fuss—perhaps only Shun managed it.”

sentences.zh: 改正朔，易服色，以順天命而已；

translations.literal: Correct the first month of the year, change garment color, thereby to comply with Heaven’s mandate, that is all;
translations.idiomatic: Shun merely fixed the calendar and court dress to answer Heaven;

sentences.zh: 其餘盡循堯道，何更為哉！

translations.literal: for the rest he wholly followed Yao’s way—what more change was there!
translations.idiomatic: the rest followed Yao’s path unchanged.

sentences.zh: 故王者有改制之名，亡變道之實。

translations.literal: Therefore the king has the name of reforming institutions, but no reality of changing the Way.
translations.idiomatic: So kings may “reform” rites, yet never touch the Way itself.

sentences.zh: 然夏上忠，殷上敬，周上文者，所繼之救，當用此也。

translations.literal: Yet Xia honored loyalty, Yin honored reverence, Zhou honored pattern—the succor they inherited required using these.
translations.idiomatic: Xia stressed plain good faith, Shang awe, Zhou refinement—each repaired what the last age had spoiled.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：「殷因於夏禮，所損益可知也；

translations.literal: Confucius said: “Yin inherited Xia ritual—the subtractions and additions can be known;
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, “Shang took Xia’s rites—what it dropped or added can be known;

sentences.zh: 周因於殷禮，所損益可知也；

translations.literal: Zhou inherited Yin ritual—the subtractions and additions can be known;
translations.idiomatic: Zhou took Shang’s rites—again the shifts can be traced;

sentences.zh: 其或繼周者，雖百世可知也。」

translations.literal: he who may succeed Zhou—even after a hundred generations it can be known.”
translations.idiomatic: and whoever follows Zhou, even a hundred reigns later, can be foretold.”

sentences.zh: 此言百王之用，以此三者矣。

translations.literal: This saying is that the use of the hundred kings takes these three.
translations.idiomatic: He meant every dynasty’s tool kit comes down to those three shifts.

sentences.zh: 夏因於虞，而獨不言所損益者，其道如一而所上同也。

translations.literal: Xia inherited Yu; alone it does not speak of subtractions and additions because its Way was one and what it honored was the same.
translations.idiomatic: Xia took Yu’s legacy whole, so the Annals name no tweaks—the Way stayed one.

sentences.zh: 道之大原出於天，天不變，道亦不變，是以禹繼舜，舜繼堯，三聖相受而守一道，亡救弊之政也，故不言其所損益也。

translations.literal: The great origin of the Way comes from Heaven; Heaven does not change, the Way also does not change—therefore Yu succeeded Shun, Shun succeeded Yao, the three sages received in succession and guarded one Way; there was no government of rescuing flaws—therefore it does not speak of their subtractions and additions.
translations.idiomatic: The Way springs from Heaven; Heaven is changeless, so the Way is changeless—Yao, Shun, and Yu handed on one thread with no “repair” edicts, hence silence on additions or cuts.

sentences.zh: 繇是觀之，繼治世者其道同，繼亂世者其道變。

translations.literal: From this viewed: he who succeeds an ordered age, his Way is the same; he who succeeds a chaotic age, his Way changes.
translations.idiomatic: Who follows peace may keep the same Way; who inherits chaos must change the recipe.

sentences.zh: 今漢繼大亂之後，若宜少損周之文致，用夏之忠者。

translations.literal: Now Han succeeds after great chaos; it seems fitting slightly to reduce Zhou’s literary polish and employ Xia’s loyalty.
translations.idiomatic: Han comes after total ruin; it should trim Zhou’s ornament and recover Xia’s plain loyalty.

sentences.zh: 陛下有明德嘉道，愍世欲之靡薄，悼王道之不昭，故舉賢良方正之士，論議考問，將欲興仁誼之林德，明帝王之法制，建太平之道也。

translations.literal: Your Majesty has bright virtue and fine ways, pities the age’s desire becoming thin, grieves that the kingly way is not shining clear; therefore you raise worthy and upright men, discuss, debate, examine, and inquire, intending to raise the forest virtue of benevolence and righteousness, clarify the laws and institutions of emperors and kings, and establish the way of great peace.
translations.idiomatic: Your luminous virtue grieves for a drifting age and a half-hidden royal road, so you summon the worthy and upright to debate policy—aiming to grow the grove of humaneness and duty, set out true kingship, and build the road to great peace.

sentences.zh: 臣愚不肖，述所聞，誦所學，道師之言，廑能勿失耳。

translations.literal: Your servant is foolish and unworthy, narrates what he has heard, recites what he has studied, conveys the words of his teachers—only able not to lose them.
translations.idiomatic: I am unworthy: I repeat what I was taught and hope not to garble my masters.

sentences.zh: 若乃論政事之得失，察天下之息耗，此大臣輔佐之職，三公九卿之任，非臣仲舒所能及也，然而臣竊有怪者。

translations.literal: If it comes to discussing gain and loss in governing affairs, examining the world’s rest and exhaustion—that is the duty of great ministers assisting and supporting, the charge of the three dukes and nine ministers—not what your servant Zhongshu can reach; yet your servant privately has what is strange.
translations.idiomatic: Weighing policy, measuring the realm’s pulse—that belongs to the high ministers, not to me—yet one thing puzzles me.

sentences.zh: 夫古之天下亦今之天下，今之天下亦古之天下，共是天下，古以大治，上下和睦，習俗美盛，不令而行，不禁而止，吏亡奸邪，民亡盜賊，囹圄空虛，德潤草木，澤被四海，鳳皇來集，麒麟來游，以古准今，壹何不相逮之遠也！

translations.literal: As for the world of antiquity also being the world of today, the world of today also being the world of antiquity—they share being the world; antiquity used it to achieve great order, high and low harmonious, customs beautiful and flourishing, without command yet acting, without prohibition yet stopping, clerks had no treachery and deviance, the people had no robbers and bandits, prisons were empty, virtue moistened plants and trees, bounty covered the four seas, phoenixes came to gather, unicorns came to roam—using antiquity to measure today, how is it that they do not reach so far!
translations.idiomatic: Antiquity’s world is today’s world—yet antiquity knew great peace: custom fair, orders needless, jails empty, omens kind, phoenix and unicorn at court. Match that to now—why the chasm?

sentences.zh: 安所繆□而陵夷若是？

translations.literal: Wherein is the error that we slide down thus?
translations.idiomatic: What twist in the road has brought us so low?

sentences.zh: 意者有所失於古之道與？

translations.literal: Perhaps there is something lost in the way of antiquity?
translations.idiomatic: Have we dropped some thread of the old Way?

sentences.zh: 有所詭於天之理與？

translations.literal: Something twisted in the pattern of Heaven?
translations.idiomatic: Or twisted Heaven’s pattern?

sentences.zh: 試跡之於古，返之於天，黨可得見乎。

translations.literal: Try tracing it in antiquity, turning it in Heaven—perhaps it can be seen.
translations.idiomatic: Search the past and scan Heaven’s logic—then the cause may show.

sentences.zh: 夫天亦有所分予，予之齒者去其角，傅其翼者兩其足，是所受大者不得取小也。

translations.literal: As for Heaven, it also apportions gifts: to those it gives teeth it removes horns; to those it fits with wings it pairs two feet—this is that what receives the large cannot take the small.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven parcels gifts with a sparing hand: hornless where teeth are sharp, two-legged where wings grow—great endowments do not stack small ones.

sentences.zh: 古之所予祿者，不食於力，不動於末，是亦受大者不得取小，與天同意者也。

translations.literal: Those whom antiquity granted emoluments did not eat from physical toil, did not stir in petty ends—this too is that receiving the large one cannot take the small, agreeing with Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Ancient stipendiaries lived on their salary alone, not on trade or manual labor—Heaven's rule that great gifts forbid petty pickings.

sentences.zh: 夫已受大，又取小，天不能足，而況人乎！

translations.literal: For one who has already received the large to again take the small—Heaven cannot make it sufficient, how much less man!
translations.idiomatic: Grab the high stipend and still scrape for coin, and neither Heaven nor earth can fill your purse.

sentences.zh: 此民之所以囂囂苦不足也。

translations.literal: This is why the people clamor and suffer insufficiency.
translations.idiomatic: That is why the people groan they can never get enough.

sentences.zh: 身寵而載高位，家溫而食厚祿，因乘富貴之資力，以與民爭利於下，民安能如之哉！

translations.literal: Their persons favored and bearing high position, their households warm and eating thick emoluments, relying on the capital of wealth and honor to fight with the people for profit below—how can the people keep up with them!
translations.idiomatic: They ride rank and salary to squeeze the market against commoners—how should the poor compete?

sentences.zh: 是故眾其奴婢，多其牛羊，廣其田宅，博其產業，畜其積委，務此而亡已，以迫蹴民，民日削月浸，浸以大窮。

translations.literal: Therefore they multiply their slave girls and boys, increase their oxen and sheep, widen their fields and houses, enlarge their estates and industries, store their hoards and piles, striving at this without cease, pressing and treading on the people; the people are daily pared and monthly soaked, gradually reaching great destitution.
translations.idiomatic: So they stack slaves and herds, swell fields and storehouses, pile hoards without end, and trample the people—who shrink month by month into utter want.

sentences.zh: 富者奢侈羨溢，貧者窮急愁苦；

translations.literal: The rich are extravagant and overflowing; the poor are pressed, urgent, worried, and bitter;
translations.idiomatic: The rich wallow in excess while the poor choke on desperation;

sentences.zh: 窮急愁苦而不上救，則民不樂生；

translations.literal: pressed, urgent, worried, and bitter, yet there is no rescue from above—then the people do not delight in life;
translations.idiomatic: and when no help comes from the throne, men cease to love life;

sentences.zh: 民不樂生，尚不避死，安能避罪！

translations.literal: when the people do not delight in life, still less do they avoid death—how can they avoid crime!
translations.idiomatic: men who no longer cherish life will not shrink from death, let alone from crime.

sentences.zh: 此刑罰之所以蕃而奸邪不可勝者也。

translations.literal: This is why punishments multiply and treachery and deviance cannot be overcome.
translations.idiomatic: Hence penalties multiply and vice cannot be stamped out.

sentences.zh: 故受祿之家，食祿而已，不與民爭業，然後利可均布，而民可家足。

translations.literal: Therefore households that receive emoluments eat the emoluments only, do not compete with the people in enterprise—then profit can be evenly spread and the people can be sufficient in each household.
translations.idiomatic: Salary families should live on salary alone and leave trade to others—then wealth spreads and every hearth can thrive.

sentences.zh: 此上天之理，而亦太古之道，天子之所宜法以為制，大夫之所當循以為行也。

translations.literal: This is the pattern of High Heaven and also the way of high antiquity; what the Son of Heaven ought to take as model to make institutions, and what great officers ought to follow as conduct.
translations.idiomatic: That is Heaven's pattern and antiquity's rule—law for the emperor, conduct for his ministers.

sentences.zh: 故公儀子相魯，之其家見織帛，怒而出其妻，食於捨而茹葵，慍而拔其葵，曰：「吾已食祿，又奪園夫紅女利乎！」

translations.literal: Therefore when Gongyi Xiu was minister of Lu, when he went to his house and saw cloth being woven, angrily he expelled his wife; eating at an inn and chewing mallow, vexed he pulled up the mallow, saying: "I already eat emoluments—shall I again seize profit from the gardener and the red-clad woman!"
translations.idiomatic: Gongyi Xiu, as Lu's minister, divorced his wife for weaving at home, yanked the gardener's greens from his bowl, crying, "I already draw a state salary—I will not snatch the craftsman's or the weaver's living."

sentences.zh: 古之賢人君子在列位者皆如是，是故下高其行而從其教，民化其廉而不貪鄙。

translations.literal: The worthy men and gentlemen of antiquity who stood in ranks were all thus; therefore those below honored their conduct and followed their teaching, the people were transformed by their integrity and were not greedy and base.
translations.idiomatic: Every worthy who held office did the same, so the people looked up, learned restraint, and shed petty greed.

sentences.zh: 及至周室之衰，其卿大夫緩於誼而急於利，亡推讓之風而有爭田之訟。

translations.literal: Reaching the decline of the house of Zhou, its ministers and great officers were slack in righteousness and urgent in profit; they lost the wind of yielding and had lawsuits over fighting for fields.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhou decayed, its nobles chased profit, forgot deference, and sued one another over fields.

sentences.zh: 故詩人疾而刺之，曰：「節彼南山，惟石巖巖，赫赫師尹，民具爾瞻。」

translations.literal: Therefore the poet hated them and satirized them, saying: "High is that southern mountain, only the rocks tower; awesome is the Master Yin—on you the people all gaze."
translations.idiomatic: The Odes lash them: "Lofty southern hills, sheer stone peaks—awe-inspiring Minister Yin, all eyes are fixed on you."

sentences.zh: 爾好誼，則民鄉仁而俗善；

translations.literal: If you love righteousness, then the people turn to benevolence and customs are good;
translations.idiomatic: If you love right, the people lean toward kindness and manners turn fair;

sentences.zh: 爾好利，則民好邪而俗敗。

translations.literal: if you love profit, then the people love deviance and customs are ruined.
translations.idiomatic: if you love profit, they turn to crooked ways and manners rot.

sentences.zh: 由是觀之，天子大夫者，下民之所視效，遠方之所四面而內望也。

translations.literal: From this it is viewed: the Son of Heaven and great officers are what those below gaze on and imitate, what distant regions from the four sides look inward to expect.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor and ministers are the mirror the people hold up; the four quarters watch them for a sign.

sentences.zh: 近者視而放之，遠者望而效之，豈可以居賢人之位而為庶人行哉！

translations.literal: Those near look and release themselves to it; those far gaze and imitate it—how can one occupy the position of a worthy man and act the conduct of commoners!
translations.idiomatic: Neighbors copy what they see, frontiers echo it—how may a man sit as a sage and walk as a plebeian?

sentences.zh: 夫皇皇求財利常恐乏匱者，庶人之意也；

translations.literal: As for anxiously seeking wealth and profit, always fearing want and exhaustion—that is the intent of commoners;
translations.idiomatic: Scheming for every coin is the commoner's mind;

sentences.zh: 皇求仁義常恐不能化民者，大夫之意也。

translations.literal: anxiously seeking benevolence and righteousness, always fearing inability to transform the people—that is the intent of great officers.
translations.idiomatic: fearing only that you cannot teach the people is the minister's mind.

sentences.zh: 《易》曰：「負且乘，致寇至。」

translations.literal: The Changes says: "Bearing burden yet riding in a carriage brings robbers."
translations.idiomatic: The Classic of Changes warns, "He who porter's load rides in a carriage invites robbers."

sentences.zh: 乘車者君子之位也，負擔著小人之事也，此言居君子之位而為庶人之行者，其患禍必至也。

translations.literal: Riding in a carriage is the position of the gentleman; bearing burden is the affair of the small man—this says that occupying the gentleman's position yet doing the conduct of commoners, its disaster and misfortune surely arrive.
translations.idiomatic: The carriage is the gentleman's seat; the shoulder pole is the laborer's tool—trade places and bandits will come.

sentences.zh: 若居君子之位，當君子之行，則捨公儀休之相魯，亡可為者矣。

translations.literal: If one occupies the gentleman's position and should act the gentleman's conduct, then aside from Gongyi Xiu's ministering Lu, nothing need be done.
translations.idiomatic: Play the gentleman's part as Gongyi Xiu did in Lu, and nothing more is required.

sentences.zh: 《春秋》大一統者，天地之常經，古今之通誼也。

translations.literal: The Chunqiu's exalting great unity is the constant warp of Heaven and earth, the pervasive righteousness of antiquity and present.
translations.idiomatic: The Annals' praise of "great unity" is Heaven and earth's fixed rule and history's common thread.

sentences.zh: 今師異道，人異論，百家殊方，指意不同，是以上亡以持一統；

translations.literal: Now teachers differ in the Way, men differ in discourse, the hundred schools diverge in direction, fingers and meanings are not the same—therefore those above have no means to hold one unity;
translations.idiomatic: Today every master teaches a different doctrine, every school its own angle—so the throne cannot enforce one standard;

sentences.zh: 法制數變，下不知所守。

translations.literal: laws and institutions change repeatedly; those below do not know what to keep.
translations.idiomatic: laws flicker and shift, and the people lose their footing.

sentences.zh: 臣愚以為諸不在六藝之科孔子之術者，皆絕其道，勿使並進。

translations.literal: Your servant foolishly thinks: as for those not within the category of the Six Arts and the arts of Confucius, all block their paths and do not allow them to advance together.
translations.idiomatic: I urge you to bar every path that is not the Six Classics and Confucius' teaching from advancing alongside the true Way.

sentences.zh: 邪辟之說滅息，然後統紀可一而法度可明，民知所從矣。

translations.literal: When deviant and perverse doctrines are extinguished and stilled, then the binding thread can be one and laws and measures can be clear, and the people will know what to follow.
translations.idiomatic: Silence heterodox schools, and the thread runs straight, the law stands clear, and the people know where to walk.

sentences.zh: 對既畢，天子以仲舒為江都相，事易王。

translations.literal: When the replies were finished, the Son of Heaven made Zhongshu minister of Jiangdu, serving King Yi.
translations.idiomatic: When the answers were done, the emperor named Dong Zhongshu chancellor to the king of Jiangdu.

sentences.zh: 易王，帝兄，素驕，好勇。

translations.literal: King Yi was the emperor's elder brother; by nature arrogant, he loved bravery.
translations.idiomatic: King Yi was the emperor's elder brother—proud by nature and fond of brawling.

sentences.zh: 仲舒以禮誼匡正，王敬重焉。

translations.literal: Zhongshu with ritual and righteousness corrected him; the king respected and honored him.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhongshu set him right with ritual and duty, and the king treated him with deep respect.

sentences.zh: 久之，王問仲舒曰：「粵王勾踐與大夫洩庸、種、蠡謀伐吳，遂滅之。

translations.literal: After a long time the king asked Zhongshu, saying: "The Yue king Goujian, with his great officers Xie Yong, Zhong, and Li, plotted to attack Wu and thereby destroyed it.
translations.idiomatic: One day the king asked, "Goujian of Yue and his ministers Xie Yong, Wen Zhong, and Fan Li destroyed Wu by counsel.

sentences.zh: 孔子稱殷有三仁，寡人亦以為粵有三仁。

translations.literal: Confucius praised Yin as having three benevolent men—I, too, think Yue had three benevolent men.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius called Shang's three men humane; I say Yue had three as well.

sentences.zh: 桓公決疑於管仲，寡人決疑於君。」

translations.literal: Duke Huan resolved doubts with Guan Zhong; I resolve doubts with you."
translations.idiomatic: Duke Huan trusted Guan Zhong with every doubt; I would trust you the same."

sentences.zh: 仲舒對曰：「臣愚不足以奉大對。

translations.literal: Zhongshu replied, saying: "Your servant is foolish and not sufficient to undertake the great reply.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhongshu answered, "I am too dull for so weighty a question.

sentences.zh: 聞昔者魯君問柳下惠：『吾欲伐齊，何如？』

translations.literal: I have heard that formerly the lord of Lu asked Liuxia Hui: 'I desire to attack Qi—how about it?'
translations.idiomatic: I recall the lord of Lu asking Liuxia Hui whether he should attack Qi.

sentences.zh: 柳下惠曰：『不可。』

translations.literal: Liuxia Hui said: 'It cannot.'
translations.idiomatic: The sage said no.

sentences.zh: 歸而有憂色，曰：『吾聞伐國不問仁人，此言何為至於我哉！』

translations.literal: Returning home he had a worried look and said: 'I have heard that attacking a state is not asked of a benevolent man—why did these words reach me!'
translations.idiomatic: At home he darkened with shame: "Humane men are never consulted on invasion—why were those words put to me?"

sentences.zh: 徒見問耳，且猶羞之，況設詐以伐吳乎？

translations.literal: He was only asked, and still was ashamed—how much more if one set deceit to attack Wu!
translations.idiomatic: He had only been asked, yet blushed—how much more men who used fraud to crush Wu!

sentences.zh: 由此言之，粵本無一仁。

translations.literal: From this speaking, Yue fundamentally did not have one benevolent man.
translations.idiomatic: By that measure Yue never had a single true man of humaneness.

sentences.zh: 夫仁人者，正其誼不謀其利，明其道不計其功。

translations.literal: As for the benevolent man, he corrects his righteousness and does not scheme for profit, clarifies his Way and does not reckon achievement.
translations.idiomatic: The humane man sets duty straight and ignores profit, clarifies the Way and scorns mere success.

sentences.zh: 是以仲尼之門，五尺之童羞稱五伯，為其先詐力而後仁誼也。

translations.literal: Therefore at the gate of Zhongni, boys of five chi's height are ashamed to name the Five Hegemons, because they put deceit and force first and benevolence and righteousness after.
translations.idiomatic: Even a child at Confucius' gate is ashamed to praise the Five Hegemons—they put fraud and force ahead of kindness and right.

sentences.zh: 苟為詐而已，故不足稱於大君子之門也。

translations.literal: If it is only deceit, therefore it is not worth naming at the gate of the great gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: Pure cunning wins no place among great men.

sentences.zh: 五伯比於他諸侯為賢，其比三王，猶武夫之與美玉也。」

translations.literal: The Five Hegemons, compared to other marquises, were worthy; compared to the Three Kings, they are like a rough warrior compared to fine jade."
translations.idiomatic: The hegemons surpassed other lords, yet beside the Three Kings they look like a brawler beside polished jade."

sentences.zh: 王曰：「善。」

translations.literal: The king said: "Good."
translations.idiomatic: The king said, "Well spoken."

sentences.zh: 仲舒治國，以《春秋》災異之變推陰陽所以錯行，故求雨，閉諸陽，縱諸陰，其止雨反是；

translations.literal: Zhongshu ordered the state, using the disasters and prodigies of the Chunqiu to infer why yin and yang ran awry; therefore to seek rain he closed off the yang and released the yin; to stop rain was the reverse;
translations.idiomatic: As chancellor he read the Annals' portents to track yin and yang; for rain he blocked yang and freed yin, for drought the opposite;

sentences.zh: 行之一國，未嘗不得所欲。

translations.literal: practiced in one state, never did he fail to obtain what he desired.
translations.idiomatic: and in each kingdom the weather answered as he asked.

sentences.zh: 中廢為中大夫。

translations.literal: In the middle he was dismissed and became Palace Grandee.
translations.idiomatic: Later he was demoted to palace grandee.

sentences.zh: 先是遼東高廟、長陵高園殿災，仲舒居家推說其意，草稿未上，主父偃候仲舒，私見，嫉之，竊其書而奏焉。

translations.literal: Before this, the Gaomiao in Liaodong and the hall of the Gaoyuan park at Changling caught fire; Zhongshu at home inferred and explained their meaning; the draft was not yet submitted when Zhufu Yan visited Zhongshu, saw it privately, envied him, stole his document and presented it.
translations.idiomatic: Before that, lightning struck the Gaozu shrines; Dong drafted an interpretation at home. Zhufu Yan peeked at it, envied him, stole the draft, and sent it up.

sentences.zh: 上召視諸儒，仲舒弟子呂步舒不知其師書，以為大愚。

translations.literal: The emperor summoned and showed it to the various scholars; Zhongshu's disciple Lü Bushu, not knowing it was his teacher's writing, deemed it supremely foolish.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor showed it to the scholars; his own pupil Lü Bushu, not recognizing his master's hand, called it absurd.

sentences.zh: 於是下仲舒吏，當死，詔赦之，仲舒遂不敢復言災異。

translations.literal: Thereupon he handed Zhongshu to the clerks, liable for death; an edict pardoned him; Zhongshu then did not dare again speak on disasters and prodigies.
translations.idiomatic: Dong was jailed for a capital crime, then pardoned—and never again lectured the throne on omens.

sentences.zh: 仲舒為人廉直。

translations.literal: Zhongshu as a man was incorrupt and straight.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhongshu was upright and austere.

sentences.zh: 是時方外攘四夷，公孫弘治《春秋》不如仲舒，而弘希世用事，位至公卿。

translations.literal: At that time they were outwardly repelling the four Yi; Gongsun Hong studied the Chunqiu not as well as Zhongshu, yet Hong catered to the age and wielded power, his position reaching the three dukes and ministers.
translations.idiomatic: The court was busy driving the barbarians back; Gongsun Hong knew the Annals less well than Dong yet flattered his way to the highest posts.

sentences.zh: 仲舒以弘為從諛，弘嫉之。

translations.literal: Zhongshu took Hong as practicing flattery; Hong envied him.
translations.idiomatic: Dong despised him as a sycophant, and Hong hated Dong in return.

sentences.zh: 膠西王亦上兄也，尤縱恣，數害吏二千石。

translations.literal: The king of Jiaoxi was also the emperor's elder brother; especially wanton, he repeatedly harmed two-thousand-bushel clerks.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Jiaoxi was another imperial brother—wild and fond of ruining his governors.

sentences.zh: 弘乃言於上曰：「獨董仲舒可使相膠西王。」

translations.literal: Hong then spoke to the emperor, saying: "Only Dong Zhongshu can be sent as minister to the king of Jiaoxi."
translations.idiomatic: Hong told the emperor, "Only Dong Zhongshu is fit to chancellor Jiaoxi."

sentences.zh: 膠西王聞仲舒大儒，善待之。

translations.literal: The king of Jiaoxi heard that Zhongshu was a great scholar and treated him well.
translations.idiomatic: The king had heard Dong was a great scholar and treated him with courtesy.

sentences.zh: 仲舒恐久獲罪，病免。

translations.literal: Zhongshu feared that if long he would incur guilt; he took illness and resigned.
translations.idiomatic: Fearing eventual punishment, Dong pleaded illness and quit.

sentences.zh: 凡相兩國，輒事驕王，正身以率下，數上疏諫爭，教令國中，所居而治。

translations.literal: In all he ministered to two kingdoms, always serving arrogant kings; he corrected himself to lead those below, repeatedly submitted memorials remonstrating and disputing, taught and ordered within the state—wherever he dwelt was ordered.
translations.idiomatic: He served two proud kings, yet by personal integrity, remonstrance, and clear orders he kept each domain in order.

sentences.zh: 及去位歸居，終不問家產業，以修學著書為事。

translations.literal: When he left office and returned to dwell, to the end he did not ask about household estates and enterprises, taking cultivation of learning and composition of books as his task.
translations.idiomatic: Retired, he never fussed over property but gave himself to study and writing.

sentences.zh: 仲舒在家，朝廷如有大議，使使者及廷尉張湯就其家而問之，其對皆有明法。

translations.literal: When Zhongshu was at home, if the court had great deliberations, it sent envoys and Commandant of Justice Zhang Tang to his house to ask him; his replies all had clear pattern.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever the court faced a great decision, envoys and Zhang Tang came to his door, and every answer he gave was lucid and grounded in law.

sentences.zh: 自武帝初立，魏其、武安侯為相而隆儒矣。

translations.literal: From the time Emperor Wu first was established, the Marquises of Weiqi and Wu'an were ministers and exalted the scholars.
translations.idiomatic: Since Emperor Wu's accession, Weiqi and Wu'an had raised Confucian learning at court.

sentences.zh: 及仲舒對冊，推明孔氏，抑黜百家。

translations.literal: Reaching Zhongshu's reply to the documents, he extended and clarified the Kong clan and suppressed and dismissed the hundred schools.
translations.idiomatic: Dong's policy answers exalted Confucius and set the hundred schools aside.

sentences.zh: 立學校之官，州郡舉茂材孝廉，皆自仲舒發之。

translations.literal: Establishing officials for schools, commanderies and prefectures recommending outstanding talent and filial and incorrupt—all issued from Zhongshu.
translations.idiomatic: Imperial academies and the commandery system of "filial and incorrupt" nominations began with his memorials.

sentences.zh: 年老，以壽終於家，家徙茂陵，子及孫皆以學至大官。

translations.literal: In old age he ended his years at home; the household moved to Maoling; sons and grandsons all through learning reached high office.
translations.idiomatic: He died of old age at home; the clan moved to Maoling, and sons and grandsons rose to high rank through scholarship.

sentences.zh: 仲舒所著，皆明經術之意，及上疏條教，凡百二十三篇。

translations.literal: What Zhongshu wrote all clarified the meaning of the classics and arts, together with memorials and itemized teachings—in all 123 pieces.
translations.idiomatic: His writings—mostly elucidating the classics, with memorials and edicts—number one hundred twenty-three pieces.

sentences.zh: 而說《春秋》事得失，《聞舉》、《玉杯》、《蕃露》、《清明》、《竹林》之屬，復數十篇，十餘萬言，皆傳於後世。

translations.literal: And discourses on gain and loss in Chunqiu affairs—such as *Wenju*, *Jade Cup*, *Fanlu*, *Qingming*, *Bamboo Grove*, and the like—again several tens of pieces, more than a hundred thousand words, all transmitted to later generations.
translations.idiomatic: His essays on the Annals—titles such as *Wenju*, *Jade Cup*, *Luxuriant Dew*, *Qingming*, and *Bamboo Grove*—add dozens more chapters and over a hundred thousand words, all handed down.

sentences.zh: 掇其切當世施朝廷者著於篇。

translations.literal: Pick out what was cutting and appropriate to the age and applied at court and write it in this chapter.
translations.idiomatic: This chapter records what was most urgent for the state.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：劉向稱：「董仲舒有王佐之材，雖伊、呂亡以加，管、晏之屬，伯者之佐，殆不及也。」

translations.literal: The encomium says: Liu Xiang declared: "Dong Zhongshu had the stuff of a king's assistant—even Yi Yin and Lü Wang would not surpass him; as for Guan Zhong and Yan Ying, assistants to hegemons, probably do not reach him."
translations.idiomatic: The historian's praise quotes Liu Xiang: "Dong Zhongshu could have served a true king—Yi Yin and Lü Wang would not outshine him; Guan and Yan, mere tools of hegemons, fall short."

sentences.zh: 至向子歆以為：「伊、呂乃聖人之耦，王者不得則不興。

translations.literal: Reaching Xiang's son Xin, he thought: "Yi Yin and Lü Wang are mates of the sage; the true king does not rise without obtaining them.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xin answered his father: "Yi Yin and Lü Wang are the sage's matched pair—a king cannot rise without them.

sentences.zh: 故顏淵死，孔子曰『噫！

translations.literal: Therefore when Yan Hui died, Confucius said 'Ah!
translations.idiomatic: When Yan Hui died, Confucius cried, "Alas!

sentences.zh: 天喪余。』

translations.literal: Heaven has bereft me."
translations.idiomatic: Heaven is killing me."

sentences.zh: 唯此一人為能當之，自宰我、子贛、子游、子夏不與焉。

translations.literal: Only this one man could match them—Ziwo, Zigong, Ziyou, and Zixia were not among them.
translations.idiomatic: Only Hui could stand beside them—not Zaiwo, Zigong, Ziyou, or Zixia.

sentences.zh: 仲舒遭漢承秦滅學之後，《六經》離析，下帷發憤，潛心大業，令後學者有所統壹，為群儒首。

translations.literal: Zhongshu met Han after Qin's destruction of learning; the Six Classics were split apart; he let down the curtain, roused his mind, and sank his heart in the great enterprise, causing later scholars to have something by which to unify—first among the throng of scholars.
translations.idiomatic: Dong lived when Qin had shattered learning; he drew the curtain, burned with purpose, and gave later scholars a single thread to follow—chief among the Confucians.

sentences.zh: 然考其師友淵源所漸，猶未及乎游、夏，而曰管、晏弗及，伊、呂不加，過矣。」

translations.literal: Yet examining the gradualness of the depth of his teachers and friends, he still had not reached Ziyou and Zixia, yet to say Guan and Yan did not reach him, Yi and Lü did not surpass him—excessive."
translations.idiomatic: Yet weighed against the lineage that produced Ziyou and Zixia, to call Guan and Yan his inferiors and rank him with Yi and Lü is going too far."

sentences.zh: 至向曾孫龔，篤論君子也，以歆之言為然。

translations.literal: Reaching Xiang's great-grandson Gong, a man who firmly judged gentlemen, he held Xin's words to be correct.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xiang's great-grandson Liu Gong, a fair critic, sided with Liu Xin.
